sections.title: Back to School from Holidays
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with students sharing holiday experiences from four different regions of India — a beach in Odisha (Chandni), the desert of Rajasthan (Ritika), the agricultural plains of Amritsar, Punjab (Gurpreet), and the mountains of Sikkim (Nayan). This narrative frame establishes the four landforms — plains, desert, coastal region, and mountains — that the chapter explores. Students are invited to connect their own travel or local experiences to these diverse environments.

tasks.task_text: You might have visited some places during your vacation. Write the name of the places and something special about them. Ask two or three of your friends about the places they had visited, and fill the table given below. Which type of place do you find interesting? What similarities and differences do you find in the place you visited, and those visited by your friends?

sections.title: In the Land of Agriculture, with Gurpreet
sections.content_summary: This section describes the fertile plains of Amritsar, Punjab, where rich soil supports farming and agriculture is the main livelihood. Students observe a picture of everyday life in this region — people farming, using tractors and animals, wearing traditional Punjabi attire. Gurpreet shares cultural details: the popular food (makki di roti, sarson da saag, lassi), and the iconic Golden Temple (Sri Harmandir Sahib) with its langar sewa — a community kitchen serving free meals to all — illustrating the spirit of collective service rooted in Sikh tradition.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture above and write your observations: 1. What kind of land do you see in the picture? 2. List the activities that people are engaged in. 3. Which animals do you see in the picture? 4. Do you notice anything unique about the clothing of the people?
What is the most popular food in your region? Can you list the main ingredients used in its preparation? Name of the food item: ___ Ingredients: ___

sections.title: In the Land of Endless Sand, with Ritika
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the desert landform through Ritika's experiences in Rajasthan. Deserts are characterised as dry places with very little rainfall; drought-resistant plants like cactus, khejri, and babool thrive there by storing water or surviving on minimal moisture. Desert animals include camels (the ship of the desert), lizards, wild cats, pythons, and eagles. The cultural dimension is rich: colourful clothing (ghaghras, bandhini dupattas, turbans), folk music instruments (khartal, sarangi), and traditional houses made of mud with thatched roofs and tankas (water tanks) for water storage. Popular desert foods include dal-bati-churma and ker sangri. The Jaisalmer Fort — built of golden sandstone — is highlighted as a heritage landmark.

tasks.task_text: Look at the following picture and write your observations. 1. What kind of land do you see? 2. What types of plants do you see in the picture? How are they different from the plants you see in your locality? 3. Is there something unique about the way people in this village are dressed? 4. How do you think people travel in deserts? 5. What do you like the most in the picture?
Ask your family members about the folk songs and dances from your region, and note them down in the table below.
Why do you think water is scarce in the desert?

sections.title: On the Seashore, with Chandni
sections.content_summary: Chandni's visit to Puri, Odisha introduces the coastal region as a landform. Coastal areas are characterised by beaches, fishing as a primary livelihood, and unique marine biodiversity including sea turtles, crabs, dolphins, corals, seaweed, and starfish. Puri is known for sand art, shankh (conch shell) used in religious rituals, the Shree Jagannath Temple, and the grand Rath Yatra chariot festival. Traditional Odishan foods — dalma, pakhala, chhena poda, rasagola — and cultural forms like Odissi dance and Sambalpuri sarees reflect the region's rich heritage. The section also covers the challenge of cyclones in coastal Odisha and highlights the Government of Odisha's award-winning disaster management practices.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture given below and write your observations. 1. List the different kinds of activities that the people are doing. 2. What kinds of trees are found near the sea, and how are they different from those in the deserts and plains? 3. List the fun activities you would like to do on a beach. 4. What is special about the dresses people wear in the coastal regions?

Use one coastal item to create a fun decor object like a photo frame, a necklace, a painted pebble, a mini basket or a sand art jar. Add your own creative twist and display your work in the class! You can use any other locally available materials to create the decor items.
Write your observations on how the life around a coastal region differs from the life in a desert.
Why is it important to keep our beaches clean?
Can you identify these creatures? (sea turtle, crab, dolphin, corals, seaweed, starfish — match names to pictures)
What are the adverse effects of a cyclone?
How can communities be better prepared for such situations?

sections.title: In the Mountains, with Nayan
sections.content_summary: Nayan's diary entries from Gangtok, Sikkim introduce the mountain landform. Mountains are described as rocky landforms that are cold, often snow-capped (Himalayan peaks), with steep slopes and valleys. Sikkim's biodiversity includes yaks and mountain animals; trees like oak, pine, walnut, and chestnut grow there. The culture of Sikkim is rich: traditional dress (bakhu, dumdyam, Gunyo cholo), food (thukpa, sel roti), festivals (Loosong and Namsoong — the Sikkimese New Year with chaam mask dance), and traditional wooden houses with slanting roofs suited to heavy rainfall. The section also covers the natural hazard of landslides in mountainous regions and community resilience.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture above and write your observations. 1. What type of animals do you see in the picture? 2. How are the dresses worn by people living in the mountains different from those worn in your region? 3. Describe the trees found in the mountains.
How does the cold climate in mountain regions affect the way people live and dress?
What traditional clothes are worn in your region? How do they match with the local climate?
Why do you think certain trees, like pine and oak, grow better in the mountains?
How do animals like yak survive in the cold climate of Sikkim?
Why do you think traditional houses in Sikkim are built with wood and have slanting roofs?
Does your house have any feature that is similar to the houses in the mountains?
Why are landslides common in mountains during the rainy season?
What do you think can be done to keep people safe during the landslides?
What can communities do to help people who lose their homes or get affected by natural disasters?

sections.title: Life in my Landform
sections.content_summary: This synthesis section asks students to reflect on and creatively represent the landform of their own region, connecting the personal to the geographical. Students draw their local landform and describe its people, professions, food, houses, plants, and animals. A comparative table consolidates learning across all four landforms (plains, desert, coastal, mountains) across categories: trees, animals, housing, clothing, food, festivals, and art forms. A final creative challenge asks students to design an imaginary landform combining favourite features and reflect on how those features benefit living beings and what challenges they might pose.

tasks.task_text: Draw the landform around you in the space given below. Name the area, the type of landform, some interesting features of the people, their professions, food, houses, plants and animals.

Compare life in the mountains and plains by capturing the differences in trees, animals, houses, clothes, food, festivals and art forms. Fill the table with columns: Plains, Desert, Coastal, Mountains.
Design and draw a landform by combining your favourite features from mountains, plains, coasts, and deserts.
What made you choose different features for your landform?
How does each feature benefit people, animals or plants?
What difficulties might people face living in your landform?

dual_strand.natural: Diverse landforms of India — plains, deserts, coastal regions, and mountains — and their characteristic climate, flora, and fauna
dual_strand.human_cultural: How landforms shape the food, clothing, housing, festivals, art forms, and livelihoods of people living in each region
